data.title: BlackBerry: The Company Has Bounced, Built Back Better
data.description:
Investment thesis

Once a formidable player in the multibillion-dollar mobile phone business, BlackBerry Limited (NYSE: NYSE:BB) saw its fortune and prospects take a significant hit. The struggles in taking on Samsung and Apple saw the company lose touch with consumers, even when producing some of the most sought-after and secure mobile phones.

After years of struggles and underperformance, BlackBerry has rediscovered itself. Its stock rallying by more than 100% last year and gaining 56% year to date is a testament to growing confidence in BlackBerry's long-term prospects and growth metrics.

BlackBerry's redesigned focus is the secret to its comeback. Having struggled to compete on the hardware side of the business, the company has revitalized its growth prospects with a focus on software. Consequently, it has become a major force in the Internet of Things ("IOT") and cybersecurity. With the industry prospect to be analyzed below, as well as the current under-valuation, I give the company a Buy rating.

Company background

Cybersecurity accounts for the biggest share of BlackBerry's core business with a $435 million annual run rate and a 55% gross margins.

Internet of Things, with a $245 million annual run rate, generates about 80% in gross margins, with IP licensing coming in third with a $20 million annual run rate and 60% gross margins.

Its cutting-edge driver-assistance platforms and infotainment systems are setting the standard for connected cars in the future. As autonomous driving technology advances, BlackBerry's software platform is becoming increasingly popular with automakers who want strong safety measures.

The company boasts a robust cybersecurity solution, embedded systems, and tracking technology portfolio. Under the leadership of John Chen, the Canadian tech giant is solely focused on reshaping the digital future through software solutions.

QNX Software Growth Prospects

QNX is one of the company's fastest-growing business segments, providing highly secure software for automobiles, medical devices, robotics, and industrial embedded systems. As of the fourth quarter of last year, QNX software was in over 255 million vehicles and more than 68% of electric cars. The fact that 24 of the top 25 electric vehicle manufacturers use the software affirms how well-positioned BlackBerry is for the next ten years as cloud computing becomes crucial for driving a software-defined future.

The Canadian company plans to offer a platform for automobiles that combines a large portion of the various computer hardware and software inside cars. John Wall, the COO of QNX, claims that this innovation will allow the company to increase its clients' average revenue significantly. BlackBerry might also consider charging subscription fees for services like security-related upgrades that can be added to car computer systems via over-the-air updates.

For the quarter that ended November, QNX unit's revenue was up 13% to $62 million compared to the previous quarter. EBITDA excluding some items also increased 38% to $18 million, affirming robust growth. BlackBerry plans to expand QNX's exposure to other sectors to generate more revenue beyond the automotive space.

"We see a lot of opportunity, especially with some of the bigger players in the industrial automation market. Robotics is an exciting market," QNX COO John Wall said.

Part of the QNX expansion plan will revolve around a strategic partnership with India's Pi Square Technologies. The two plan to provide a free, non-commercial version of its development platform to establish QNX as a standard in the automotive and IOT software sectors.

Secure Communications Lucrative Contracts

Secure Communications is BlackBerry's second business segment, which provides security technologies for protecting data and communications networks. It offers SecuSUITE, a cybersecurity solution that protects networks from various cybersecurity threats. Consequently, BlackBerry has secured deals with government agencies, strengthening its revenue base.

BlackBerry's Secure Communications portfolio includes the crucial events management platform AtHoc, the most popular product in the federal government and utilized by numerous federal agencies. It also provides SecuSmart, an encrypted voice solution that the US military, government agencies, and the Secret Service utilize. Lastly, the data sent to individual smartphones is managed by the company's Unified Endpoint Management ("UEM") solution.

Revenue in the Secure Communications segment has been growing steadily as BlackBerry reaps the rewards of its ongoing contract with financial services companies and government agencies.

BlackBerry Radar is one of BlackBerry's leading software solutions focused on providing advanced asset-tracking technology. It's also tailored to enhance efficiency across logistics and supply chain networks. The solution increasingly strengthens the Canadian company's growth prospects in the multibillion-dollar supply chain industry.

BlackBerry is also pursuing growth opportunities around quantum and blockchain technologies. It has already started integrating blockchain technology to enhance supply chain networks. It also leverages quantum computing to advance the encryption of its software solutions. Focusing on the two advanced technologies could be the catalyst for strengthening the company's growth metrics in the tech sector and strengthening its prospects in the market.

Improving Profit Margins

The sale of Cylance's endpoint security assets to Arctic Wolf for $160 million positions BlackBerry to focus on its core Secure Communication business. The fact that the segment consists of BlackBerry UEM, BlackBerry AtHoc, and BlackBerry SecuSUITE means the Canadian company will continue servicing its lucrative contracts with government agencies and financial services.

Similarly, the sale relieves BlackBerry of dealing with a unit that generated negative earnings of about $50 million annually. Given that the Secure Communication unit generated a positive EBITDA of $50 million, BB remains well positioned to strengthen its profit margins following the divestment of the sale of Cylance, and the additional money raised should enable the company to keep expanding its IoT operations and look for possible acquisitions that might improve its standing in the market.

BlackBerry is already considered a profitable company following the sale of Cylance. The company generated positive earnings from continuing operations of $12 million for the November quarter, expected to improve following the sale.

The QNX software business is also expected to impact profit margins positively. The unit's backlog has grown at a 33% compound annual growth rate. The company's Chief Financial Officer, Tim Foote, has already indicated that the significant backlog levels will bolster the company's profits in the next two to three years.

BlackBerry Revitalized Growth Prospects

BlackBerry's transition from a popular mobile phone company to a heavyweight in cybersecurity and automotive is gaining traction. The strategic shift places it at the forefront of emerging technologies, especially cybersecurity, automotive, and Internet of Things domains.

With a history of successful innovations, BlackBerry is leveraging its rich history to redefine its future on the software front. Ongoing developments and strategic partnerships highlight its potential to be a significant player in the future tech landscape. Likewise, the drive for safe, connected technologies worldwide underlines BB's stock as a viable option for long-term growth.

BlackBerry's market projections point to a bright future. Its AI-based cybersecurity solutions are becoming more popular due to increased demand for strong security measures and worldwide cybersecurity threats. BlackBerry's improving profit margins, robust growth outlook on the QNX unit, and the potential benefits of its Secure Communications business affirm long-term prospects.

Valuation

With the growth prospects in mind, we compare Blackberry with its peers in both the cybersecurity sector and the SaaS sector. In terms of multiples, Blackberry is still trading as an emerging, early software company. It's P/S ratio is significantly lower than the leaders in cybersecurity, despite having a growing positive cash flow and decent EBITDA.

With this in mind, the one-year target P/S ratio is at around 7x-10x, while the EV/EBITDA ratio is at around 25x-30x. As such, my target price for the company ranges from $5.9 - 6.7, which has an upside of more than 15%.

Investment risks

  • Technology and competition: In the cybersecurity field, competitions are fierce. Existing players such as PaloAlto Network and CloudFlare are established in the sector and competing with them requires time and resources, which may not yield effective results
  • Geopolitical tensions: BlackBerry is developing its business in China as discussed. With the new administration being unpredictable in foreign trade policy, and the retaliation from China, cross-border businesses like Blackberry may be impacted
  • Liquidity concerns: The company has a debt to cash ratio of lower than 1, signaling that it may face liquidity issues should the company needed more investment in its new strategy

data.title: Kratos Stock Is Flying Too High, Time To Sell Before The Bubble Bursts
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My investment thesis is very simple: Kratos (NASDAQ:KTOS) is both the most expensive company on the market and among its competitors in terms of P/E and EV/EBITDA, and at the same time, it is one of the lowest–margin companies in the industry, with weak business growth rates, unstable financial situation, and value growth prospects - this simply cannot be.

  • The average P/E (TTM) among competitors is 58x, while Kratos' P/E (TTM) is as much as 272x;
  • The average EV/EBITDA (TTM) among competitors is 19x, while Kratos EV/EBITDA (TTM) is as much as 44x;

I believe that the company's shares need to be shorted, and their fair value is 34% lower than current values. I am assigning Kratos a "Sell" rating with a target price of $18 per share.

Introduction

Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, Inc. is a large military technology company engaged in the development and manufacture of advanced systems and solutions for the U.S. Department of Defense and for the commercial sectors. The key activity of Kratos is the creation of unmanned aircraft systems (UAS), hypersonic and missile technologies, cybersecurity, satellite communications and advanced military solutions. Kratos specializes in providing innovative, but at the same time low-cost solutions, which makes it a niche company in the field of developing solutions for the American military-industrial complex. The company develops and implements new technologies for the U.S. Department of Defense, intelligence agencies, NASA, as well as major defense contractors and technology companies.

Kratos' business model is as follows: the company develops, manufactures and sells high-tech products and software for the needs of the defense and commercial sectors. The company's revenue is generated through government contracts, commercial orders, and long-term program agreements. The main sources of revenue include fixed production contracts (70% in 2023), actual cost and commission contracts (24%), and time and materials contracts (6%). 69% of the company's total revenue in 2023 was provided by orders from the US Department of Defense, NASA, the Missile Defense Agency, the Space Command and other government agencies, while the remaining 31% was provided by large private companies.

Kratos' development strategy is aimed at strengthening its position in key technology segments, expanding partnerships with major defense contractors, and increasing its share in fast-growing areas such as hypersonic technology, cybersecurity, satellite systems, and autonomous combat platforms. The company adheres to the "first to market" strategy, striving to be a leader in the development of advanced technologies and provide competitive advantages due to low-cost and fast development cycle. Kratos is also actively expanding its international presence: in 2025, it is planned to open new production facilities in Israel and India, which will strengthen its position in the global defense technology market.

Competitors

I believe that a comprehensive analysis of the company cannot be carried out without examining the industry itself in which Kratos operates. The company is considered to belong to the Aerospace and Defense industry. However, the industry itself is too vast and has a large number of segments within it. If we talk about the industry as a whole, the Aerospace and Defense Industry can be described as an oligopoly with elements of monopolistic competition in certain segments. It is important to understand that the main client of this industry is the government represented by the US Department of Defense, NASA, the Missile Defense Agency, the US Intelligence Agency and other major defense contractors. This industry has a limited number of large players, which is typical of an oligopoly, but it is divided into segments where smaller players operate. The market leaders are Northrop Grumman, Lockheed Martin, General Dynamics, Raytheon Technologies, BAE Systems, L3Harris, Boeing, General Atomics and others. These companies have significant financial, technological and production resources, which makes it extremely difficult for new players to enter the market. Kratos is not able to fully compete with these giants. Government orders are often awarded through tenders, but due to high requirements, contracts with long-term commitments, and the specifics of national security, the market remains highly concentrated in the hands of several companies.

At the same time, Kratos competes in certain segments such as hypersonic technologies, autonomous unmanned systems, satellite solutions, and electromagnetic weapons. Monopolistic competition operates in these segments. The main competitors here are Spirit AeroSystems Holdings, AeroVironment, Intuitive Machines, Hexcel Corporation, and in fact these are the key competitors of Kratos. If we summarize all these companies as one segment from the Aerospace and Defense industry, then this entire segment can be estimated at 10.47 billion dollars. Kratos' share in this segment is estimated at only 11%, while the largest player is Spirit AeroSystems with a 62% share.

The company's main competitive advantage lies in lower production costs and faster development time for new solutions. Kratos focuses on more affordable and mass-scale solutions. This gives it a niche competitive advantage, but competition remains fierce, as large defense contractors have the financial and technological resources to displace smaller players from certain niches in the long run. As a result, Kratos remains a weak player both in the overall industry and in its individual segments, lacking pricing power, and therefore Kratos is unable to increase its marginality and business growth rates at the expense of competitors.

Thus, the US defense industry is an oligopoly in which several large corporations control the main military programs and production of equipment. However, there is an element of monopolistic competition in innovative sectors such as unmanned systems, hypersonic technologies, and satellite communications, where Kratos offers more affordable and flexible solutions. This gives the company the opportunity to grow, but also exposes it to the risk of being ousted by more powerful players.

Financials

In the third quarter of 2024, the company's financial performance grew modestly compared to the third quarter of 2023. The company also revised its forecasts for key financial statements in a negative direction, especially in terms of operating profit. Management explains the deterioration in the forecast by a combination of several factors, including delays in deliveries, rising personnel costs and the impact of the Continuing Resolution Authority (CRA), which limits the possibility of obtaining new contracts and increasing financing for current ones.

According to the results of the third quarter, revenue increased by 0.5% compared to the same period in 2023 and amounted to $275.9 million. Revenue dynamics across the company's various business lines were mixed. The main contribution to revenue growth was made by the Unmanned Systems (KUS) division, which increased its revenues by 8.7% to $64.2 million in 2024. Other business areas of the company also showed positive dynamics, however, this growth was significantly offset by a drop in revenue in the space and satellite segment, where revenues decreased by $24.2 million. The reason for the drop in revenue is delays on the part of OEMs in the production and supply of software-defined satellites, which slowed down the receipt of new orders and the implementation of existing contracts.

The average annual growth rate of total revenue (CAGR) between 2018 and 2024 was about 10%. Management expects revenue growth of 10% in 2025, which is in line with my forecast. I expect revenue to grow by 10.91% to 1.275 billion rubles. The main contribution to growth is likely to come from large long-term contracts already concluded in 2024, which ensure a stable flow of orders. In particular, this is a 1.45 billion MACH-TB 2.0 contract.

The cost of sales includes the cost of production, SG&A, and 2-3% of all revenue the company directs to R&D. The cost increase in 2024 was mainly due to investments in technology development, staff growth, and high costs for equipment and customer support.

More and more of the company's costs are accounted for by R&D because the company continues to develop its product line and increase the staff of engineers. As a result, I expect the company to maintain its R&D spending level of about 3% of total revenue in 2025.

Kratos management expects EBITDA in the range of $102-107 million by the end of the year, which coincides with my forecast of $104 million in 2024. At the same time, I expect a reduction in EBITDA by the end of 2025 due to a reduction in EBITDA margin to 7.44%. My expectations are supported by the weak growth rate of the company's business, increased staff and R&D costs, as well as the company's position in the market, which does not allow it to keep margins at a stable level (as I said, the company's market share is small, there are many competitors, and they are strong, and Kratos does not have pricing power). The average annual EBITDA growth rate from 2018 to 2024 is 13.67%. According to my forecast, the company is likely to earn about $94.8 million in EBITDA in 2025.

Net profit for the year 2024 is likely to amount to about $15-16 million, potentially jumping by 553% year-on-year. In my opinion, the company would be able to increase its net profit margin to 1.3% from 0.23% in 2023 due to new contracts, particularly the MACH-TB 2.0 contract. And the return on equity (ROE) is about 1.34%. I expect net profit to grow to $21 million in 2025. Thus, we see that the company is running a very low-profit business, which may tell us either that management is not developing the business efficiently, or that the market is so competitive that Kratos has to sell its developments as cheaply as possible.

Reducing the debt burden remains the only positive development for the company. By the end of 2024, Debt to Equity decreased to 0.30x, indicating a decrease in dependence on borrowed funds and a strengthening of the capital structure. Net Debt/EBITDA, which peaked at 8.32x in 2022, decreased to negative values, which confirms a significant improvement in the financial situation. The Interest Coverage Ratio remains at a comfortable level of 1.51x, demonstrating sustainability in the payment of interest expenses. The growth of the Current Ratio to 3.22x highlights the increased liquidity and financial flexibility, which can allow the company to continue investment programs without risks to financial stability. The weighted average interest rate on the company's loans depends on the leverage level and amounts to SOFR + 1.25%-2.25%. To reduce interest rate risks in 2023, Kratos fixed the rate at 3.721% through a swap contract valid until May 2026.

Thus, Kratos' financial position is stable, but there are no great prospects for improvement. In fact, the company's business is low-margin and at the same time requires a constant increase in investments in R&D in order to remain competitive. Yes, indeed, the company has deleveraged over the past 3 years and significantly reduced its debt burden, but this business definitely does not look like P/E 272x from a financial point of view.

Valuation

Despite the fact that Kratos is shamelessly expensive by all multipliers, many analysts still recommend this promotion for purchase. It's a mystery to me how you can acquire a fairly mediocre business for 272 profits. And even despite the recent 20% stock price correction, Kratos remains the most expensive company in the industry and in its segment. Today, Kratos is trading at a 42% premium to its historical P/E averages and 7% on the EV/EBITDA multiplier. My thesis is that the company is inadequately expensive, no matter what you compare it to – peers or historical averages.

For comparison, I took Kratos' key competitors in the segment of developing cheap drones, or companies relatively similar to it from the Aerospace and Defense industry. In terms of P/E (TTM), Kratos is the most expensive among all, and in terms of EV/EBITDA, it is one of the most expensive. At the same time, it is one of the lowest-margin companies in the industry with the lowest growth rates.

The key advantage remains the absence of net debt, whereas some competitors have a significantly higher debt burden. At the same time, the company also shows one of the lowest ROE (1.34%), which indicates inefficient capital management. Collectively, Kratos remains an overvalued asset with low business growth rates, low margins, and controversial drivers for development.

In my forecast of the target price, I use three estimation methods: DCF (estimated weight – 50%), target value of P/E and target value of EV/EBITDA with weights (estimated weights of 25%). In the basic DCF valuation scenario, I assume a growth rate of 3% in the terminal period and a weighted average cost of capital of 10.26%.

The target P/E multiplier for 2025 is 58x, and EV/EBITDA is 19.5x (I took the average values for competitors). According to my calculations, the final fair valuation of Kratos shares on the horizon of the year is $18 per share, which is 34% lower than the current price. Thus, I consider the company's shares to be highly overvalued at the moment and I believe that it is possible to look closely at the purchase only at a price below $18 per share.

Risks

In order to make my thesis about the revaluation of this company more convincing, I will list the key risks that the company may face and which may negatively affect the business and the price of its shares, respectively.

A key risk for Kratos is dependence on contracts with the U.S. government, which provide most of the revenue. Any changes in budget policy, cuts in defense spending, or delays in allocating funding could negatively affect Kratos' business, reduce new orders, and lead to revenue losses. In addition, the continued use of temporary budget resolutions (CRAs), which prohibit the conclusion of new contracts and increased funding for existing programs, poses a serious threat to business stability. In addition, the impact of politics, for example, possible budget changes under the new US president, can significantly change the balance of power among companies in the industry. The geopolitical tension and the war in Ukraine favored industry companies to earn more money due to the growth of the defense budget, as well as through the popularization of drones. Against this background, Kratos shares increased significantly by almost 4 times, and revenue increased by 41% during this period. However, with the coming to power of Donald Trump, who intends to reduce a large number of programs, including those related to the military-industrial complex, the geopolitical situation may calm down, and the war in Ukraine might come to an end sooner than expected. We see all these steps after the start of direct negotiations between the United States and Russia to defuse bilateral relations and establish peace in Ukraine. Trump also stated that he would propose to Russia and China to halve defense spending. All this may mean a reduction in revenue for companies in the industry, including Kratos. It is this risk that is currently the key for the company, and the probability of realizing this risk is extremely high.

High competition in the defense industry also poses a significant challenge. Kratos competes with both large system integrators such as Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman and Raytheon Technologies, as well as smaller innovative companies that are actively implementing new technologies. High competition leads to lower product prices, lower margins and a loss of market share, which we actually see in the company's financial statements.

Thus, each of the risks I have listed can negatively affect the stock price. At the same time, these risks are already partially realized, as a result of which the market reacts with a correction in the share price of Kratos.

Drivers for stock price growth

Despite all the risks I have listed, Kratos still has strengths that the company can take advantage of and that can boost its stock price. The main factor is the growth of the defense budgets of the United States and its allies, which leads to an increase in demand for unmanned systems, satellite technologies and cyber defense. Despite Trump's peaceful rhetoric, negotiations may fail, and the war in Ukraine may continue, while increasing competition with China may force the US government to continue building up defense budgets.

Recent major military operations have shown that drones are a key tool for effective modern warfare. It is highly likely that the largest countries will continue to invest in and expand their unmanned programs. The company has already demonstrated success in developing next-generation drones such as the XQ-58A Valkyrie and Air Wolf, which can be used in combat operations without risking pilots. Kratos participates in the Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA) program, where the US Air Force is considering the possibility of large-scale deployment of unmanned fighter jets. If the company receives large contracts under this program, its shares can grow significantly.

The financial stability of Kratos is also an important argument for the growth of its shares. The company demonstrates stable revenue growth, improves profitability and reduces its debt burden, which makes it attractive for investment. In addition, it is actively involved in the process of consolidating the defense industry: Kratos can either become a takeover target for a large defense corporation, or acquire promising companies on its own, which can increase its strategic importance.

Conclusion

Thus, I believe that the share price of Kratos is extremely overvalued, even after a 20% drop, and I disagree with most analysts about the serious growth potential of the company's business. I recommend selling the company's shares and considering its possible purchase only at a price level below $18. Kratos is currently the most overvalued company in the sector, and at the same time the company with the lowest profitability and business growth rates. The company has significant risks that can negatively affect the business and its final assessment, so keeping Kratos or even buying it at this price is a big risk.

data.title: Snowball Your Passive Income: 2 High-Yield Dividend Stocks With Significant Growth
data.description:
Investing in dividend growth stocks is a great way to not only compound your wealth and achieve attractive total returns over the long term, but perhaps even better, it is a great way to achieve financial independence and sustain that financial independence over the long term. This is because if you can live off of your dividend income, you reduce your sequence of returns risk since dividends are much more stable and dependable than stock market performance, which can be very volatile from day to day, month to month, and year to year.

One of the reasons why dividend growth stocks are such a great way to invest for total returns is because companies that pay out attractive dividends are typically very profitable and also need to have a stable business model to be able to support that dividend. On top of that, if they grow their dividends year after year, it means that management is very effective at allocating capital because it is able to support paying out increasing distributions to shareholders over time. It is fairly easy to paint a much better picture than what is actually happening at a company with all sorts of fancy non-GAAP metrics, but actually delivering increasing amounts of cold, hard cash to shareholders year after year is something that cannot be faked. Additionally, a rising dividend payout typically means that earnings per share are growing as well, and therefore, intrinsic value per share is increasing. This tends to drive the stock price higher over time, which, combined with the attractive dividend payout, delivers strong total returns.

On the financial freedom front, when you combine an attractive current payout that meets your current expenses with a high dividend growth rate, your purchasing power not only tends to keep up with inflation but actually increases relative to inflation over time. This means that your standard of living can actually improve during retirement if you follow this strategy.

While some might describe the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) as a simple, diversified path to achieving financial freedom through dividend growth investing, we think there is a better way to do it. For sure, SCHD is a fine approach for passive investors who do not enjoy the art of investing, but it is best suited for those who do not want to manage their own portfolios, study companies, or value stocks. However, for those who want to achieve better total returns and higher current income, we believe that either supplementing SCHD with some individual high-yield, high-growth stocks or even building an entire portfolio of individual stocks, as we do, is the best approach.

I wrote more about this in a recent article, so for more detail on that, you can refer to it and some other high-growth, high-yield stocks here. But in the meantime, here are two high-growth, big-dividend stocks that, I believe, are great vehicles for a portfolio that, with or without SCHD, can help you achieve your financial independence and total return goals over the long term.

High-Growth Big Dividend Stock #1

The first one I am going to talk about today is Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP). BEP is a renewable power production and development company that is well-diversified across a broad range of technologies and geographies.

While many have put renewable power-and BEP by extension-in the penalty box right now due to President Trump's declared aversion to wind power, the reality is that wind only makes up about one-fifth of BEP's total portfolio, as well as its development pipeline. Additionally, only about half of its FFO comes from the United States. Therefore, a significant portion of its wind business is actually outside of the U.S., and nearly half of its cash flow comes from its crown jewel hydro portfolio.

As a result, BEP is much more than a wind power company. In fact, it is much more than even a wind and solar company, as combined they only make up 37% of its total FFO. Instead, it is a global, diversified energy giant that generates a large portion of its cash flow from real estate-like, permanent hydro assets that are currently benefiting from strong recontracting tailwinds. On top of that, it has a high-growth nuclear reactor business and is also poised to benefit significantly from the AI boom. It has a major power purchase agreement signed with Microsoft (MSFT) to power its AI ambitions, and is signaling that it expects to sign similar deals with other hyperscalers in the future. Management is also seeing further upside potential to its deal with MSFT.

Combining that with its BBB+ credit rating, its $4.3 billion in available liquidity, an 11-year weighted average term to maturity on its debt, and only 9% of its debt having recourse to the corporation-with the rest being asset-level non-recourse debt-it is clear that BEP is in a strong financial position. Additionally, its lengthy contract profile along with the fact that the vast majority of its cash flow is indexed to inflation, makes it well-positioned to thrive in a stagflationary environment.

Perhaps most exciting of all is that management expects a 10%+ annualized FFO per unit CAGR for the foreseeable future, driven by a combination of its contractual rent escalators, contractual power purchase agreement escalators, operational improvement plans, extensive development pipeline, and capital recycling program. BEP has also proven to be opportunistic in repurchasing its own units and remains dedicated to growing its distribution year after year at a pace that easily exceeds inflation, which it will likely continue to do for the foreseeable future.

When you combine its double-digit FFO per unit CAGR outlook with its 6.4% distribution yield, BEP's total return and income potential become quite clear. Compared to other blue-chip real asset businesses such as Realty Income (O), the yield is somewhat comparable, as O's dividend yield is near 6%. However, BEP's growth rate is more than double that of O's, making it a much more dynamic investment. Meanwhile, its defensive positioning is nearly as strong, its credit rating remains solid, and it is also backed by one of the world's largest alternative asset managers, Brookfield (BN)(BAM).

On top of that, it offers investors flexibility through both its K-1 issuing security - BEP - as well as an economically equivalent option through Brookfield Renewable Corporation (BEPC) for those who wish to avoid the K-1.

High-Growth Big Dividend Stock #2

Another high-growth, big-dividend stock that I like a lot right now is Amcor (AMCR). It is a diversified packaging company that is preparing to merge with Berry Global (BERY), a move expected to enhance its capabilities, scale, and supply chain. Ultimately, this merger aims to deliver substantial synergies and accelerated growth through their highly complementary portfolios and innovation platforms. As a result, management expects 35% adjusted cash earnings per share accretion by year three, they anticipate $650 million in synergies, an additional $280 million of one-time cash benefits, and ultimately $3 billion of annual cash flow available to reinvest in the business, on top of the $180 million R&D spend.

Revenue growth is expected to accelerate by at least 1% from current levels, and total annualized return expectations are projected to increase by 300 basis points annually. As illustrated in management's capital allocation plan, they expect to distribute capital roughly equally across three different avenues:

  1. $1 billion toward capital expenditures for reinvestments into the existing business to drive strong organic growth.
  2. $1 billion into acquisitions and share repurchases, allocated to the most opportunistic places based on market conditions.
  3. A little over $1 billion in annual dividend payouts. This dividend will likely increase over time as the company raises it, although much of that increase will likely be offset by share repurchases. As a result, investors can combine the company's current 5% dividend yield with its expected 10% to 15% EPS CAGR to achieve 15% to 20% annualized total shareholder returns.

Management believes the stock will eventually reprice to a 3% to 4% dividend yield over time, providing further upside potential for investors. When you factor in its strong investment-grade balance sheet-with leverage expected to fall to three times or below by the end of fiscal 2025-significant liquidity, and strong free cash flow generation, AMCR is competitively positioned with a well-diversified business model and strong growth potential and looks like a very dynamic dividend growth investment.

Investor Takeaway

As this article discusses, dividend growth investing is a powerful way to compound wealth over the long term and achieve financial freedom. Fortunately, there are plenty of opportunities in the market today that combine attractive current dividend yields with strong dividend and EPS growth potential. These are the kinds of stocks we are loading up on in our portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Organigram Is A Small-Cap Bargain
data.description:
I have been writing extensively about Organigram (NASDAQ:OGI) on the Seeking Alpha website for almost two years now. I first called it out as the best cannabis stock in May 2023 when it was $0.47. It is much higher now, but this is due to the reverse-split! Most recently, I wrote about it in early December, ahead of its Q4 report, saying it could soar in 2025. The stock, now down 18.6% year-to-date, is down 13% since that article. In this follow-up, which is part of the Seeking Alpha Best Small/Micro Cap Idea competition, I explain why it has dropped and why my outlook remains so bullish. At 19.7%, it is the largest position at this time in my model portfolio at 420 Investor.

Organigram Stock Is Down a Lot

Organigram plunged after it reported its fiscal Q1 earlier this month. It had run up in advance following the Aurora Cannabis (ACB) fiscal Q3 report a few days earlier:

The six-month chart shows that a new recent low of $1.24 was set on 2/21, as it closed at $1.31. Six months ago, the stock was above $2.

Looking at the action over the last 2 years, one can see the multi-year low set below $1 in late 2023 and then the huge run-up afterwards to almost $3:

The peak that was set in late March last year was followed by a surprise equity offering that caused a gap in trading and then a massive decline. The stock did recover a bit last summer, but it gapped down again after the fiscal Q1 report, which I discuss below. So, there are 2 open gaps above. I see support at $1.15-1.25 based on the action in late 2023, and I see a lot of resistance.

All cannabis stocks have been under pressure. Looking back to the end of 2020, ahead of the peak in cannabis stocks in February 2021 and ahead of the British American Tobacco (BTI) investment initially in March 2021, Organigram has dropped 75.4%. It is currently a member of the New Cannabis Ventures Global Cannabis Stock Index, which has declined 85.9% since the end of 2020, so Organigram has fallen less than the cannabis sector. Comparing it to peers since then, it is down a lot less than most:

BTI invested C$221 million for 20% of Organigram, paying effectively US$12.13 per share. The stock is almost 90% lower now! British American Tobacco has invested more in the company at this lower price, a deal announced last November as a C$124.6 million purchase over three closings at a price of C$3.22, which is currently US$2.26. Two of the closings have taken place, in January 2024 and in September, and the next one should close this week. The company reported in September that BTI owns 30% of the common stock and all of its preferred shares, which could allow it to boost the ownership to 49%. As of 2/6, BTI owns 8.5 million preferred shares and 32.5 million common shares, the total of which represents 25% of the fully diluted shares outstanding. In a few days, we will be updated with the exact holdings after another investment above the current market price.

The Fundamental Story Remains Strong

Organigram reported its fiscal Q4 for the year ending 9/30/24 in December, and then its fiscal Q1 on 2/6. FY24 was a 12-month year, while FY23, a year in which they extended the fiscal year-end by one month, was 13 months.

Just ahead of the FY4 report, the company acquired Motif Labs, another Canadian LP, for C$90 million, including C$50 million in cash and C$40 million in stock (17.23 million shares). There is a C$10 million contingent payment if OGI stock exceeding a 30-day VWAP of C$3.22 12/06/25. In the FYQ1 report, there was less than a month of Motif contribution. Overall, revenue grew 17% to C$43 million, which was below expectations of C$49 million. The increase was due to international growth and the Motif acquisition. Adjusted EBITDA had been expected to be C$3 million, and it fell short as well at C$1.4 million. While it was less than had been expected, it was up a lot from a year earlier, when it was C$0.1 million.

Looking at the company's revenue, recreational flower sales rose 16% and represented 55% of net revenue. Vape sales soared due to the Motif acquisition and represented almost 10% of sales. Hash, infused pre-rolls and edibles, which fell from a year ago, represented 25% of sales. International rose 224% and represented 8% of total net revenue.

The balance sheet is very strong at Organigram, which ended Q1 with C$46.8 million cash and short-term investments and no debt. The ratio of current assets to current liabilities is a very healthy 3.3X. I was disappointed that the tangible book value fell from C$297.8 million to C$242 million due to the Motif acquisition. Cash flow from operations was -C$4.2 million, which was down sequentially and from a year ago, but this may have been due to the acquisition.

According to Koyfin, the analysts had been expecting FY25 revenue to be C$251 million ahead of the Q1 report. Now, they project that revenue will rise by 48% to C$236 million, an increase of C$76 million. Ahead of the acquisition closing, Organigram had said that Motif revenue on a trailing twelve-month basis had been C$86 million, so this accounts for more than all of the expected increase. Adjusted EBITDA in FY25 is expected to be C$10.2 million, up from FY24 but still a pretty low margin of 4.3%. For FY26, the analysts expect that adjusted EBITDA will almost double to C$20.2 million, a margin of 7.2%. The company has provided some guidance for FY25: approximate gross margin of 35%, adjusted EBITDA increasing and positive cash flow from operations.

The OGI Valuation Is Quite Cheap

Looking at the FY25 projections, the enterprise value, which is currently C$226 million, is 22X the projected adjusted EBITDA, which is no bargain on the surface. Looking at FY26, though, it trades at just 11.2X. Not bad for a federally legal cannabis company, but very high compared to the MSOs in the U.S. The comparison isn't so great, though, because the MSOs pay 280E taxation and aren't listed on the NYSE or the NASDAQ. Organigram is subject to a very unfair cultivation tax in Canada, but this could change. It is not adjusted out of adjusted EBITDA.

In the past, I liked that OGI traded at a discount to its tangible book value, but the tangible book value is lower today than it was ahead of the Motif acquisition. The stock trades at 1.1X, which is still very good.

In my last OGI piece, I shared a target of potentially C$2.59 assuming it trades at 10X adjusted EBITDA projected for FY26. I shared as well my view that it could trade at 15X a higher adjusted EBITDA (15% margin of revenue), which would be C$4.65 at that time. Since then, the company has done a substantial acquisition. I am using now 15X but applying it to the current estimate, and this gives C$2.33 (which is US$1.64). A 12% margin on the current projected revenue would give an adjusted EBITDA of C$33.6 million, which yields C$3.67 (US$2.58) with the current 150 million-fully diluted in-the-money shares outstanding. That level is slightly above the price that BTI will be paying for more shares this week, and 97% above the current price. It would also fill the gap that was created in late March last year when the company priced an equity offering.

Risks to Organigram

Having a lot of cash and no debt and a strategic investor that works well with the company mitigates a lot of risk, but Organigram does face some challenges.

The Motif acquisition could have integration issues. It was a privately held company that I did not know ahead of the deal.

The Canadian cannabis market is maturing. A record monthly sales of C$500 million was reported for December, but the growth in 2024 was just 1.5%. So much of the sales for Organigram come from this mature market. Also, the country is facing a change in leadership.

International sales opportunities abound for Organigram, but these sales are to Australia, Germany, Israel and United Kingdom. There could be issues in these markets, and the expansion into additional markets could pose a risk too.

Conclusion

Ahead of the FY25-Q1 report, I trimmed my Organigram position in my model portfolio, and I boosted it after the decline. The stock has fallen more than I would have expected and looks very attractive to me.

Finding very low valuations in the cannabis sector isn't that difficult right now, but a lot of the stocks run a big risk of 280E taxation not going away. This is not an issue for OGI.

My target is higher, and it is based on the company remaining independent. If growth in profitability improves, the stock could go a lot higher. Also, strategic partner British American Tobacco is paying almost double the current price to buy more shares directly from the company this week. The closing of the third and final transaction could attract investors. I also think it is possible that BTI could buy OGI, which has a market cap of just US$197 million.

Organigram is growing, is cheap, and is potentially an acquisition target. Of course, there are some risks, but having a strong balance sheet and low valuation offset them to a great degree. Cannabis stocks have recently posted an all-time low, but OGI continues to trade well above the multi-year low set in late 2022.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Small/Micro Cap Idea investment competition, which runs through February 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: TransMedics: Big Growth And Operational Advantages, Buy This Leading Medical Player
data.description:
Reliable and advancing medical equipment is becoming more crucial as time moves along. All healthcare fields seek that next-level innovation, and one of those in particular is organ transplantation. End-stage organ failure is sadly a consistent and growing issue worldwide.

TransMedics Group (NASDAQ:TMDX) is a medical technology company committed to revolutionizing the organ transplant industry, from procurement to management to surgery itself. Specializing in liver, lung, and heart procedures, the company provides portable warm perfusion techniques and assessment of donor organs for transplantation. Its Organ Care System [OCS] is a portable device that optimizes and monitors with customized technology to replicate near-physiologic conditions for donor organs.

TMDX became public in the spring of 2019 and hit a peak share price of $175 in August 2024. Since, the last six months have essentially been a free fall for the stock, where it now trades around $78 per share. I expect a rebound for TMDX, and I rate the stock a buy. The earnings and revenue miss from Q3 2024 has hit the stock hard, while investors also await news on an interesting short call. Above that, the company has had phenomenal growth over the last five years, with strong financials in revenue and margins both up about 15x in five years. One earnings miss is a small blip in the road, considering TMDX has developed efficient technology and systems with a competitive advantage over peers. U.S. transplants are increasing, and so is the market share and revenue on transplants for TMDX.

What’s Caused the Correction?

Q3 earnings didn’t arrive as TMDX had hoped. EPS came in at 0.12, a huge miss, and down largely from 0.35 in each of the previous two quarters. Revenue for the quarter was $108.76 million, missing estimates by $6.24 million. The company followed the poor earnings news with lowering their projected full-year revenue to $428M-432M from $425M-445M. Q3 reported a 45% drop in international revenues, and on top of that, the currently strong U.S. dollar is making exports out of the country more expensive for international customers.

Organ Care System and National OCS Program

The historical organ preservation method has been through cold storage and has big time and distance limitations. TMDX has developed its Organ Care System [OCS] as the only FDA-approved multi-organ technology platform. OCS is fully portable and has normothermic preservation and assessment technology that mimics human physiology and optimizes the organ during transport. This system allows for maximized potential of hearts, lungs, and livers through donation.

The National OCS Program is a full, end-to-end donor organ retrieval & clinical management service that successfully executes procuring and delivering healthy donor organs from anywhere in the U.S. to any transplant center. With hubs across the entire country, they provide the only air or ground transport logistics network 100% dedicated to organ transplant missions. In August 2023, TMDX completed an acquisition of Summit Aviation with strides to be a leader in air transportation of organ donation to transplantation. This past September, they purchased their 18th aircraft and have really separated themselves with a competitive edge in operational capabilities.

Day-Time Transplants

Day-time liver transplants have been a challenge for this industry for decades. They are logistically complex with procuring the liver, coordinating different regions, and sensitive to safe and healthy transportation of the organ. Through the OCS system, TMDX has gained an advantage in the sought after day-time transplants by doctors and institutions. Their 24/7 management, transportation capabilities, and hubs across the states, have allowed for OCS day-time liver transplants to quickly increase to 76%, while non-OCS has declined slightly over the last few years to 52%.

Growing Market Share

National volume in heart and liver transplants grew 12% between 2022 and 2023. For heart, liver, and lung transplants, TMDX has seen significant improvement in market share as they have been a huge driver for growth in total transplants. In 2022, TMDX accounted for 6.7% of total transplants. The total number of national transplants increased by 20% over two years stretching out to 2024, where TMDX now took care of 20.8% of those surgeries. TMDX has noted in their growth strategy that kidney transplant capabilities are being worked on for the future, as well as offering NOP operations on an international scale.

Financials

As mentioned, the Q3 earnings and revenue were a disappointment. My view is that was just a small blip in the road. The revenue and profit growth over five years is remarkable. Revenue is up 16x, and gross profit is up 15x in the TTM when compared to full year 2020. Healthcare sector YoY revenue growth average is 7.61%, while the five-year TMDX average is 93.25%. Operating profit finally flipped positive in 2024. Looking at the numbers, there is a lot to like about TMDX.

Valuation vs Technical

One of the risks to buy rating for TMDX is the obvious thought of overvaluation. The stock has a P/E ratio of 79.81, whereas the sector median is 18.04. Price to sales is the “best” valuation metric for TMDX, but at 6.17, is still almost double the industry average. The estimated forward-looking P/E ratio is expected to drop largely over the next two years. Investors will need to keep an eye on if the EPS growth can keep up with the price, I think it can. EPS was negative for years, turning positive at .07 in Q3 2023. In Q1 2024 it was .35, already 5x higher. The growth has been great, one bad report does not mean the growth will end in my opinion.

The 20-day simple moving average crossed under the 200-day on November 1, right after the unexpected miss in earnings reported in late October that resulted in the stock to plummet. You can see in the chart that there is still some room to go for that highly coveted cross over watched by technical and momentum investors, but in the last month, you can see the margin is finally beginning to tighten again.

Upcoming Earnings

I expect a rebound when Q4 earnings arrive on February 27. Historically, TMDX has beat on earnings and revenue over the last several years. The stock ran up tremendously during all of the earnings wins, and I think one good earnings report here could be a huge confidence boost for investors. Revenue guidance was dropped after last earnings, and that may signal this round won't be a huge win, but nonetheless time to get back in the win column.

Other Risk

On top of the financial misses, the stock took a further hit to start the new year in January. Scorpion Capital, a short selling activist firm, made a short call on TMDX, alleging that the company was involved in kickbacks, billing fraud and unreported device failures.

TMDX has refuted the claims as inaccurate and having no merit. Of course, this was a concern for investors and drive the stock price down even lower. The lawsuit battle is just beginning, and we can only assume will take ample time for results to get nailed down. In the meantime, since its low share price of $57 in late January, TMDX is now up 41% in the last month. It already seems like investors have moved on based on recent price movement. I think this short call is behind them, but is till something to monitor on how the lawsuit plays out.

Conclusion

While Q3 earnings left investors disappointed, I think it's important to look at the bigger picture. Since the stock made its IPO in 2019, the company has grown tremendously in so many avenues. The total U.S. transplant revenue has over doubled in the first nine months of 2024 vs 2023, as seen in the chart above. Their OCS and NOP operations are a step above the rest in the organ industry and their growth market share growth is evident. The lawsuits with Scorpion Capital are absolutely something to keep an eye on, but with no confirmation on any of the reports, I don’t expect any news on this any time soon. TMDX looks to be delivering nice results in a medical field that perhaps needed a change. I rate this stock a buy, and expect them to continue their thriving command of the organ transplant market.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Carbon Capture Is Where It's At
data.description:
Brief Thesis Walkthrough

Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY) was the large bet that Warren Buffett famously made in 2022, adding onto an already existing position since 2019. The market reacted by rallying behind OXY. Since the performance has been very solid Buffett showed markets why it's such a great opportunity to be buying domestic energy companies now.

The future looks bright for OXY and due to the attractive share price and recent positive results I am rating the company a buy, with a high likelihood of raising this in the short term should operations continue to impress.

OXY Market Position And Current Market Climate

OXY is a larger energy company included in the integrated oil and gas industry. Despite being included with gas, the vast majority of revenues for OXY is derived from oil revenues, 86% from the filing. The business is divided into three parts:

Oil and Gas

Chemical

Midstream and Marketing

The first segment is quite self-explanatory, OXY explores, develops, and then produces oil and natural gas. These operations span across the globe, but the majority of the revenues are generated in the US, more specifically from its on-shore production in the Permian Basin and the Rockies. Total US revenues make up between 86 - 88% in any given quarter. This means the financial performance of OXY is heavily tied to the domestic price of oil and chemicals like chlorine, potassium hydroxide, and potassium carbonate. I include this since the second-largest segment is “Chemicals”. It is more or less responsible for the remaining 12 - 14% of revenues in any given quarter.

OXY has faced mixed opinions by the market since late January when Goldman Sachs (GS) decided to cut their rating on the business to a sell.

“Capital returns currently are a key focus for investors, but "deleveraging remains the primary focus for OXY for several years until the company is able to expand its capital returns framework”

The price target was changed from $54 per share to $45 per share. The reason provided is that OXY is in a phase of de-leveraging rather than returning capital to investors which would make it a worthwhile investment. The stock has since recovered as Warren Buffett continued to build up his position in the large US oil producer.

From a report in January, it's estimated that OXY is now the third largest US oil producer, jumping from a number five spot in 2023. Exxon Mobil (XOM) continued to hold the number one spot. XOM had 1.960.962 boe/d and OXY 1.223.169 boe/d in production. The total well count for XOM was 20.263 and 17.516 for OXY. Total active rigs were higher for OXY at 27 and 19 for XOM. The key difference between the two companies is that XOM also has a large amount of offshore production. The ranking only measured onshore production in the US. Nonetheless, in the market that OXY is in it has a large market share. I found one finding from the report very interesting, due to merger and acquisition activity in the energy sector during 2024 it has made a noticeable impact on the top 10 listing and its total production percentage. During 2023 the 10 largest companies produced 56% of all onshore oil. During 2024 that number climbed to 62% instead. OXY itself has been active on this front, closing a $12 billion deal to acquire CrownRock in August of 2024.

Oil Assets Analysis

During Q4 performance was strong which drove OCF to $3.6 billion. Total production was 1.463 Mboed which exceeded the previous guidance on the midpoint by 13 Mboed. In both midstream and chemical, the previous set guidance was exceeded.

The CrownRock integration is continuing, and it is driving significant value for OXY. It stated that prior estimates of the acquisitions have been exceeded and will deliver 5% YoY production in 2025 compared to 2024. Lower well costs will also be a bullish point for investors. If OXY can drive further margin expansion in the near term, actually expecting to save $1 MM per well across all of its Midland Basin programs, it could result in a higher multiple. Just after the announcement, the shares were up over 6% as the market took the report very well.

Across the board, assets are performing very well and with the high cash flow generated during 2024, OXY is well set up to take 2025 by storm.

OXY DAC Projects - Profiting From Saving The Planet

Legacy energy companies are facing mounting pressure to reduce their emission outputs. One effort that also holds the potential to generate revenues is carbon capture projects or CCUS. OXY has been active in these investments and specifically in the Permian Basin it has worked with carbon and storage for 40 years already.

With the STRATOS construction OXY, will have 250 KTPA in capacity by 2025. 2025 LCV net capital expenditures for the projects are expected to be $450 million. The DOE has also awarded OXY with a $500 million grant for future projects in South Texas.

DAC or “direct air capture” is still very expensive to perform.

“The cost for DAC remains incredibly high. Current costs rest somewhere in the neighborhood of $1100-$1200 per ton to capture and process CO2.”

With the true cost being between $1100 - $1200 per ton it's clearly not yet a sustainable business practice according to BlackRock.

“To tackle both cost and energy use, the DOE’s Carbon Negative Shot program, launched in 2021, ambitiously aims to get the cost of direct air capture below $100 per metric ton by sponsoring technology innovation. That price would make air capture profitable against, for example, a U.S. tax credit that, as of 2022, offers $180 per metric ton for large-scale stored CO2.”

If it's possible to reach below $100 per tonne it would present a significant business opportunity for OXY. Between 2022 and 2023 the DAC tax credit remained the same at $180 per tonne. Moving into 2024 the tax credit continued to stay the same at $180. What qualifies a business for this is that certain labor apprenticeship requirement needs to be met. If not, the DAC tax credit sinks to $36 per tonne. I have no reason to believe that OXY won't achieve this though.

The current tax credits look like this depending on the method used:

Permanent Geological Storage - $180/tonne

Enhanced Oil Recovery - $130/tonne

Utilization (includes synthetic fuels, cement) - $60/tonne

Initial capacity at the STRATOS facility is 250,000 tonnes per year, then increasing to 500,000 as the project develops. If the capture costs fall to $100 and tax credits remain the same at $180 OXY can generate $80/tonne in net income. At each step of capacity, net income would look like this:

250,000 Annual Capacity - $45 million

500,000 Annual Capacity - $90 million

What is driving the increased effort in DAC is that data centers will be a large emission contributor over the next decade. Emissions from the industry are expected to triple by 2030 and globally data centers will emit 40% of what the US emits in a single year. If governments want to reach emission goals then raising the tax credit seems like a reasonable step to motivate companies like OXY to invest in the technology.

Since 2017, there has been a large increase in carbon removal tax credit sales. According to data from CDR.fyi total deliveries reached 590,000 in February of 2025. Despite this, 13 million tons have already been sold for a total value of $3.6 billion or $276/tonne. Companies like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) are increasing spending on these efforts to reach government emission targets across the globe. In 2025 MSFT has already bought 3.3 million in credits. I highly doubt these spending efforts will slow down as more companies follow suit.

Let's take out an average between the government tax credit of $180/tonne and what the market has been paying which is $276/tonne. The average comes out to $228 and with this input, the net income for OXY could be simulated as such:

250,000 Annual Capacity - $32 million

500,000 Annual Capacity - $64 million

During 2024 OXY spent $788 million on dividend payouts ($0.88 annual dividend x 896 million outstanding shares). Allocating all net proceeds from the simulated earnings in DAC would increase the current dividend by 8.11%.

Total DAC Capacity Simulated

The total DAC capacity at STRATOS is 1 million tonnes. At $228/tonne this would net OXY $128 million in earnings. The DOE grant will add an additional 1 million in capacity to DOE once finalized, and 500,000 initially. In the press release, they state that the location in South Texas has the build-out capacity to reach 30 million tonnes per year and store 3 billion worth of carbon in saline formations. Just for fun, I figured I should include that 30 million tonnes comes out to $3.84 billion in net earnings. Keep in mind we won't see this finished in the next decade, I don't think, it's still in the early stages.

DAC Efforts Can Support Dividend Growth

For 3 years OXY has raised the dividend payable to shareholders. The 5-year growth rate is negative due to OXY suspending the dividend between 2020 and 2022. Between 2023 and 2024 the quarterly dividend was raised from $0.18 to $0.22, up 22.2%. With the inflows of DAC earnings, OXY could raise it a further 8.11% in 2025 to $0.237 per share or $0.95 annually. This would mean a FWD yield of 1.97%. As OXY has more and more capacity for its DAC operations, the dividend will benefit.

Peer Group Comparison And Fair Value Assessment

In my peer group comparison, I will be looking at EOG Resources Inc. (EOG) and Devon Energy Corporation (DVN). All three companies derive a significant portion of all revenues from crude oil production, and all three have significant portions of their production in the US.

Over the past 3 years, the stock price movement of all three companies has followed a similar pattern. Shares of DVN have performed the worst and are down 35%. Shares of EOG have been in an uptrend, up 12%. For OXY the stock price movement has been stale and shares are slightly down. General market pessimism and worries about how tariffs will affect domestic markets is in my opinion why many energy stocks have had a rough last 12 months.

Market Cap

OXY - $47 billion

EOG - $75 billion

DVN - $24 billion

2025 Revenue Outlook

OXY - 16.2%

EOG - 0.3%

DVN - 1.52%

TTM EBITDA Margin

OXY - 47.6%

EOG - 55.1%

DVN - 52.4%

TTM FCF Margin

OXY - 13.6%

EOG - 18.6%

DVN - (10.8%)

FWD P/S

OXY - 1.74x

EOG - 3.15x

DVN - 1.47x

FWD Non-GAAP P/E

OXY - 14x

EOG - 11x

DVN - 7x

From the figures I have shared, I think OXY remains a very attractive buy right now. Based on revenue outlooks it's by far the fastest-growing company, much due to the CrownRock acquisitions. This acquisition has since the beginning of the integration shown a much higher ROI than previously estimated. On an EBITDA margin, OXY falls behind but now that much, 47.6% is still very strong.

On an FWD valuation basis, OXY still looks solid, trading far below EOG, for example. Based on FWD P/E it's the highest, but not far above EOG either, which trades at an 11x multiple. OXY is the high-growth business out of the peer group and the active role it has taken in the energy M&A market has meant accelerated growth. This is worth paying a premium for. Based on my estimates, OXY is fairly valued at 16x earnings and 2x sales. On average, this leaves a 16.5% upside not accounting for the dividend. My argument for these multiples is that OXY deserves a premium given its robust outlook and its early engagement in the DAC industry. Over the long term taking these early steps will secure reliable core earnings for the business.

Risks To The Thesis

I’ve talked about how a potential tax credit increase for DAC might be introduced in order to reach 2050 emission targets. Since Trump has taken office, he has backed out of the Paris Agreements. This makes it less likely that further tax credits will be introduced domestically in the US. This could halter OXY's DAC efforts and make further investments significantly less attractive as more value can be found in expanding oil production instead.

Since EBITDA is heavily tied to the price of crude oil, it can make for volatility between quarters. Depending on how well OXY keeps down operating expenses it will determine the impact falling oil prices have on the share price. During 2023 through 2024, OXY actually managed to reduce domestic lease obligations from $10.96/Boe to $8.65/Boe representing a plus 20% reduction. YoY this meant stabilized operating costs but should this trend reverse and OXY for the first time since Q4 2023 posts a QoQ increase, it would hurt investor confidence in management and the business mode.

Conclusion

Shares of OXY rallied following a very solid end to 2024. The company continues to remain an attractive bet on domestic energy production. Its carbon capture expansion opens up the way to a very reliable FCF source, which can fund further dividend increases over the years. Once again, Warren Buffett has shown why buying domestic is such a solid strategy now. I’m bullish on OXY and conclude by rating it a buy.

data.title: Ariel Investments Mid Cap Value Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Markets worldwide defied expectations in 2024 led by the dominating performance of mega-cap technology stocks known as the “magnificent seven.1” Even though gains have been concentrated, the results of the U.S. election, strong earnings momentum and a robust labor market drove bullish sentiment. This ebullience extended beyond the equity market: gold delivered its best year since 2010, bitcoin more than doubled and the U.S. dollar neared a 20-year-high. Meanwhile, uncertainty surrounding the new U.S. administration’s policies and the Federal Reserve signaling slower than anticipated easing in the year ahead drove small cap issues sharply lower in December. Although uncertainty and volatility are likely to remain elevated, the patient investor knows “stock prices trade on fundamentals. And when those solid fundamentals shine through, share prices rise. 2” Against this backdrop, the Ariel Mid Cap Value Composite delivered 0.00% gross of fees (-0.14% net of fees) for the quarter, outpacing the Russell Midcap Value Index’s -1.75% return, but falling short of the Russell Midcap Index’s +0.62% gain. Over the trailing one-year period, the Ariel Mid Cap Value Composite advanced +7.71% (+7.14% net of fees), underperforming both the Russell Midcap Value Index and the Russell Midcap Index which returned +13.07% and +15.34%, respectively.

Several stocks in the portfolio had solid returns in the quarter. Global investment bank, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) outperformed on a robust quarterly earnings beat, highlighted by strength across its investment banking, trading and asset management segments. Meanwhile, the U.S. election has been widely viewed as a positive catalyst across the industry.

Investors expect the incoming administration to emphasize deregulation and exhibit a greater openness to business combinations compared to the prior regime. Hence, management’s positive commentary around the operating momentum of its core franchises, an improving M&A outlook and the resilience of the U.S. economy sent shares higher.

Leading global financial franchise Northern Trust Corporation (NTRS) also advanced following the delivery of strong quarterly earnings results, highlighted by increasing trust fees, solid net interest income growth and margin expansion. Meanwhile, the newly appointed executive leadership team is taking actions to position the firm for profitable growth and greater operational efficiency while continuing to serve the evolving needs of its client base. In our view, NTRS remains a trusted name offering diversified products in a favorable industry with high barriers to entry.

We believe Northern’s 135-year track record highlights its strength and stability of navigating macroeconomic volatility with a conservative, operating approach.

Additionally, alternative asset manager, Carlyle Group Inc. (CG), traded higher over the quarter with the U.S. election widely viewed as a positive catalyst across the industry.

Investors expect the incoming administration to emphasize deregulation and exhibit a greater openness to business combinations compared to the prior regime. Hence, management’s positive commentary around the expectation for increased capital market activity, fee-related earnings growth, private equity fund appreciation as well as returning capital to shareholders via buybacks sent shares higher. At current levels, we believe CG trades at a healthy discount to our private market value estimate.

Alternatively, shares of Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) traded lower on mixed earnings results. Although organic revenue growth declined on lower sales volumes, solid cost containment drove a bottom-line beat. Meanwhile, SWK’s transformation initiatives remain on track. The company delivered margin expansion by realizing savings from sourcing initiatives, productivity improvements and cost efficiencies. Though the macroeconomic backdrop remains challenging, management is cautiously optimistic lower interest rates will drive consumer demand. We have conviction in SWK’s experienced executive management team and think the balance sheet is well positioned to weather the storm.

Madison Square Garden Entertainment Corp. (MSGE) was another underperformer in the quarter. Despite the delivery of strong earnings results, shares traded lower following a reduction to fiscal 2025 adjusted operating income guidance. The revision was driven by unique circumstances surrounding concert tour cancellations and higher costs associated with bringing sponsorship sales in-house.

Nonetheless, with marquee assets such as New York’s Madison Square Garden, Radio City Music Hall, Beacon Theatre and The Chicago Theater, we believe MSGE is well positioned to capitalize on strong demand for live entertainment. Additionally, new sales and renewal activity in the company’s hospitality business remains robust. MSGE recently announced multi-year sponsorship deals with Lenovo, its subsidiary Motorola Mobility, the Department of Culture and Tourism-Abu Dhabi, as well as a multi-year extension of its sponsorship deal with Verizon. In our view, MSGE’s portfolio generates stable cash flow that should enable further deleveraging. At current levels, the company is trading at a significant discount to our estimate of private market value.

Lastly, marketing communication company, Interpublic Group of Companies, Inc. (IPG) weighed on results over the period. IPG reported an earnings miss primarily due to several significant recent account losses. Subsequently, Omnicom (OMC) announced plans to acquire IPG in an all-stock deal. We believe this strategic combination will create value for both groups of shareholders by combining highly complementary data and technology platforms as well as enabling new offerings.

We did not initiate or exit any positions in the quarter.

We believe the U.S. economy looks healthy and expect corporate profits to prove resilient. Although interest rates will likely settle at a higher structural level compared to the past decade and potential policies around tariffs and immigration present uncertainty, we think market friendly policies such as tax cuts and deregulation should support the broadening of the market beyond the Magnificent Seven. Once the performance gap between mega-cap stocks and smaller company counterparts narrows, our portfolios should be rewarded. We strongly believe the disciplined investor that stays the course and consistently owns differentiated businesses with solid competitive positioning and robust balance sheets will deliver superior returns over time.

Footnotes

1 The “Magnificent Seven” are the largest stocks in the S&P 500 Index driving market performance: Apple Inc. (AAPL), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Meta Platforms Inc. (META), Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA) and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA).

2 Hobson, Mellody and John W. Rogers Jr. “What the Stock market Taught Us This Year: Don’t Fall for These Investing Traps.” Wall Street Journal, 5 December 2023.

Investing in mid-cap companies is more risky and volatile than investing in large cap companies. The intrinsic value of the stocks in which the portfolio invests may never be recognized by the broader market. The portfolio is often concentrated in fewer sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market.

Investing in equity stocks is risky and subject to the volatility of the markets.

Past performance does not guarantee future results. For the period ended 12/31/24 the performance for the Russell Midcap Value Index and the Russell Midcap Index for the 1-, 5-, and 10-year periods was +13.07%, +8.59%, and +8.10%, and +15.34%, +9.92%, and +9.63%, respectively. Ariel Composite Net of Fees returns are calculated by deducting: (1) for the period from inception to December 31, 2013, the maximum advisory fee in effect for the respective period, applied on a monthly basis; and (2) for the period from January 1, 2014 onwards, the actual monthly advisory fee (on an asset-weighted basis) accrued for the accounts in the composite, using the fee rates in place as of the most recent calendar quarter-end. Gross returns do not reflect the deduction of advisory fees. Client returns will be reduced by advisory fees and such other expenses as may be incurred in the management of the account. Advisory fees are described in Part 2 of Ariel’s Form ADV. Returns assume the reinvestment of dividends and other earnings. Returns are expressed in U.S. dollars. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. The Ariel Mid Cap Value Composite differs from its primary benchmark in that it has fewer holdings and more concentration in fewer sectors.

As of 12/31/24, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. constituted 3.5% of the Ariel Mid Cap Value Composite (representative portfolio); Northern Trust Corporation 3.6%; Carlyle Group, Inc. 2.9%; Stanley Black & Decker, Inc. 2.0%; Madison Square Garden Entertainment Corporation 2.1%; and Interpublic Group of Companies, Inc. 2.9%.
